Holland also clarified the position of the Cardiff RFC Premiership team after the rebrand, "Cardiff Rugby is the overarching brand that's going to encompass the pro team and the semi-pro team... Cardiff, as the Rags team, will play in the Premiership. There is no change with that."[[199]](#cite_note-walesonline1-199) "

"Rags" was a reference to the traditional nickname for the Cardiff second XV.[[200]](#cite_note-200) Following the rebrand, academy manager Gruff Rees became Director of Rugby of the Cardiff RFC XV and began a process Rees described as "full alignment with our academy".

Chairman Alun Jones further clarified, "What the rebrand does is to re-connect with our history. It's about having a clear identity."[[199]](#cite_note-walesonline1-199)

#### The Champions Cup "Misfits"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: The Champions Cup \"Misfits\"")]

Cardiff's 2021/22 season had begun steadily, with three wins and two close defeats in their first five URC matches. After the autumn internationals, the squad flew to South Africa for matches against the Lions and Stormers.

While there, news of the spread of the [Omicron variant](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Omicron_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant") in South Africa forced the postponement of the matches. The entire squad was forced into quarantine, initially in South Africa and later at airport hotels in England.[[201]](#cite_note-201)

Champions Cup matches against French champions [Stade Toulousain](/wiki/Stade_Toulousain "Stade Toulousain") and English champions [Harlequins](/wiki/Harlequin_F.C. "Harlequin F.C.") were now in doubt as Cardiff seemed unlikely to be able to raise a team from the few players who hadn't flown to South Africa. A total of 32 players and 10 members of staff had been on the trip and an additional 10 players were either injured or suspended.[[202]](#cite_note-202)

Cardiff made the decision to fulfill the fixtures nevertheless. Gruff Rees was put in temporary charge of the team.[[203]](#cite_note-203) In addition to the few players that had been left at home, he called upon members of the [Cardiff RFC](/wiki/Cardiff_RFC "Cardiff RFC") semi pro squad and added two experienced semi pro props from [Aberavon RFC](/wiki/Aberavon_RFC "Aberavon RFC").[[204]](#cite_note-204)

The team Rees dubbed his "misfits" therefore took the field against Toulouse at the Arms Park with a primary school teacher ([Evan Yardley](/wiki/Evan_Yardley "Evan Yardley")), groundsman ([Rowan Jenkins](/wiki/Rowan_Jenkins "Rowan Jenkins")) and recruitment consultant (Alex Everett) amongst the part time players in the 23 man squad.[[205]](#cite_note-205) Before a rowdy Arms Park crowd and Channel 4's cameras, the team performed admirably and beyond expectations before going down to an [Antoine Dupont](/wiki/Antoine_Dupont "Antoine Dupont") inspired Toulouse team.

The following week, a similar squad, now with "Rags" Backs Coach [Dan Fish](/wiki/Dan_Fish "Dan Fish") - who had only recently retired from professional rugby - at outside half played Harlequins at the Stoop.[[206]](#cite_note-206) Tries by academy players [Cameron Winnett](/wiki/Cameron_Winnett "Cameron Winnett") and [Theo Cabango](/wiki/Theo_Cabango "Theo Cabango") meant that the game was tied at 17 all at half time before Harlequins finally took control of the game away from the "misfits" in the second half.[[207]](#cite_note-207) Fish's inspired performance was widely praised in the media and sealed his status as a cult hero among Cardiff supporters.

#### Financial troubles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Financial troubles")]

In April 2025, the club indicated it was due to go into [administration](/wiki/Administration_in_United_Kingdom_law "Administration in United Kingdom law").[[208]](#cite_note-208) The assets and business were bought out of administration by the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") 24 hours later to safeguard the clubs ongoing existence. The Union intended to sell to private investors at a later stage, but for the foreseeable future would run Cardiff as a WRU subsidiary.[[209]](#cite_note-209)

## World Rugby Hall of Fame

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: World Rugby Hall of Fame")]

Cardiff are one of only three clubs to have been inducted into the [World Rugby Hall of Fame](/wiki/World_Rugby_Hall_of_Fame "World Rugby Hall of Fame").[[210]](#cite_note-halloffame-210) The club's innovation of the four three quarter system in 1885 was copied first by Wales and later spread throughout the rugby world, leaving a lasting mark upon the game. The club was inducted into the Hall of Fame in 2011.

In addition, eleven former Cardiff players have been inducted into the Hall of Fame:[[210]](#cite_note-halloffame-210)

## Colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Colours")]

The traditional Cardiff colours of blue and black were modelled on the colours used by [Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge](/wiki/Gonville_and_Caius_College,_Cambridge "Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge")[[211]](#cite_note-211) and remained in use by the team until 2006.

At that time, Cardiff Blues changed their playing strip in a decision widely interpreted as a move away from the old Cardiff RFC identity, as for the first time black was not included alongside the blue.

A variety of alternative colour designs have been used as change strips and for jerseys used in European rugby. A blue and gold jersey modelled on one worn by [Aberdare RFC](/wiki/Aberdare_RFC "Aberdare RFC") and created to raise money for the seriously injured Aberdare born Cardiff player [Owen Williams](/wiki/Owen_Williams_(rugby_union,_born_1991) "Owen Williams (rugby union, born 1991)")[[212]](#cite_note-212) proved popular and was worn in the 2018 [European Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Challenge_Cup "European Challenge Cup") final win in Bilbao.

Cardiff Rugby have now returned to their traditional colours of Cambridge blue and black, as of 2021.
[[213]](#cite_note-213)

## Home ground

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Home ground")]

Main article: [Cardiff Arms Park](/wiki/Cardiff_Arms_Park "Cardiff Arms Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Cardiff_Arms_Park_and_Millennium_Stadium.jpg/250px-Cardiff_Arms_Park_and_Millennium_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cardiff_Arms_Park_and_Millennium_Stadium.jpg)

[Cardiff Arms Park](/wiki/Cardiff_Arms_Park "Cardiff Arms Park") has been owned by Cardiff Athletic Club[[223]](#cite_note-223) since the 1920s and has long been considered Cardiff Rugby's spiritual home.[[224]](#cite_note-224) From the late 19th century onward, the Arms Park hosted both a cricket ground and a rugby ground until the southern side of the site was sold to the WRU in 1968. The northern side of the Arms Park then became Cardiff Rugby Club's home stadium, while the southern side was to become first the National Stadium and later the [Principality Stadium](/wiki/Principality_Stadium "Principality Stadium"). Cardiff have moved high-profile fixtures "next door" on a number of occasions, such as the [2008–09 Heineken Cup](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Heineken_Cup "2008–09 Heineken Cup") semi-final versus [Leicester Tigers](/wiki/Leicester_Tigers "Leicester Tigers").

The Arms Park currently consists of two main stands, both with seated and terraced sections. At the east and west ends of the stadium are blocks of hospitality facilities and office space. The Cardiff Athletic Clubhouse sits next to the South Stand.

For three seasons from the beginning of the 2009–10 season the first team moved to the new [Cardiff City Stadium](/wiki/Cardiff_City_Stadium "Cardiff City Stadium") at [Leckwith](/wiki/Leckwith "Leckwith"). Financial pressures and supporter dissatisfaction led to several home games being moved back to the Arms Park in the 2011–12 season. On 8 May 2012 it was announced that the 20-year lease with Cardiff City F.C. had been broken by mutual consent following significant financial losses incurred as a result of the move. The club returned to the Arms Park from the 2012/13 season.

For the conclusion of the abbreviated 2019/20 season, and the start of the 2020/21 season, due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") and the use of Cardiff Arms Park as part of the Dragons Heart emergency hospital, Cardiff played some home games behind closed doors at [Rodney Parade](/wiki/Rodney_Parade "Rodney Parade") in [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales").[[225]](#cite_note-225) The club also returned to Cardiff City Stadium for two matches behind closed doors during this period.[[226]](#cite_note-226)

In 2022, it was announced that an extension to the lease at Cardiff Arms Park had been signed with Cardiff Rugby's main shareholder, [Cardiff Athletic Club](/wiki/Cardiff_Athletic_Club "Cardiff Athletic Club"). Cardiff Athletic Club are exploring options for a refurbishment and redevelopment of the Arms Park stadium and wider site.[[227]](#cite_note-227)

## Regional responsibilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Regional responsibilities")]

### Clubs and Schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Clubs and Schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/WalesRugbyRegions.png/250px-WalesRugbyRegions.png)](/wiki/File:WalesRugbyRegions.png)

A map showing the Welsh rugby regions.

Cardiff Rugby are responsible for assisting the development of rugby in an area covering five [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authorities "Unitary authorities"): [City of Cardiff](/wiki/City_of_Cardiff "City of Cardiff"), the [Vale of Glamorgan](/wiki/Vale_of_Glamorgan "Vale of Glamorgan"), [Rhondda Cynon Taff](/wiki/Rhondda_Cynon_Taff "Rhondda Cynon Taff"), [Merthyr](/wiki/Merthyr "Merthyr") and southern [Powys](/wiki/Powys "Powys"). This area includes 76 community clubs, 61 secondary schools and 320 Primary schools.[[229]](#cite_note-229)

Tref-y-Clawdd RFC in [Knighton](/wiki/Knighton,_Powys "Knighton, Powys") on the border with [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") is the most northerly rugby club within this area, but the majority of clubs are based within the population centres of Cardiff and the communities to the immediate north and west of the city. They include Super Rygbi Cymru club [Cardiff RFC](/wiki/Cardiff_RFC "Cardiff RFC"), [Welsh Premiership](/wiki/Welsh_Premiership "Welsh Premiership") [Merthyr RFC](/wiki/Merthyr_RFC "Merthyr RFC") and [Pontypridd RFC](/wiki/Pontypridd_RFC "Pontypridd RFC") plus WRU Championship clubs [Beddau RFC](/wiki/Beddau_RFC "Beddau RFC"), [Rumney RFC](/wiki/Rumney_RFC "Rumney RFC"), [St.Peters RFC](/w/index.php?title=St.Peters_RFC&action=edit&redlink=1 "St.Peters RFC (page does not exist)"), [Cardiff Metropolitan University RFC](/wiki/Cardiff_Metropolitan_University_RFC "Cardiff Metropolitan University RFC"), [Glamorgan Wanderers RFC](/wiki/Glamorgan_Wanderers_RFC "Glamorgan Wanderers RFC") and [Ystrad Rhondda RFC](/wiki/Ystrad_Rhondda_RFC "Ystrad Rhondda RFC") .[[230]](#cite_note-230)[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)

The club has close links with [Coleg y Cymoedd](/wiki/Coleg_y_Cymoedd "Coleg y Cymoedd"), [Cardiff and Vale College](/wiki/Cardiff_and_Vale_College "Cardiff and Vale College"), [Ysgol Gyfun Gymraeg Glantaf](/wiki/Ysgol_Gyfun_Gymraeg_Glantaf "Ysgol Gyfun Gymraeg Glantaf") and [Whitchurch High School](/wiki/Whitchurch_High_School "Whitchurch High School"), with all four playing in the WRU's elite Schools and Colleges League.[[231]](#cite_note-231) The club has also formed a partnership with the fee paying [Christ College Brecon](/wiki/Christ_College_Brecon "Christ College Brecon")[[232]](#cite_note-232) and has formed links with [Hartpury College](/wiki/Hartpury_College "Hartpury College") in Gloucestershire.[[233]](#cite_note-233)

The area also includes [Cardiff Metropolitan University](/wiki/Cardiff_Metropolitan_University "Cardiff Metropolitan University") and [Cardiff University](/wiki/Cardiff_University "Cardiff University"), both leading University teams competing in [BUCS Super Rugby](/wiki/BUCS_Super_Rugby "BUCS Super Rugby") in addition to the [University of South Wales](/wiki/University_of_South_Wales "University of South Wales"). Cardiff Academy players are often also students at these three universities.

### Community Foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Community Foundation")]

Cardiff Rugby Community Foundation are the charitable, not for profit community arm of the organisation. The Foundation delivers a range of programmes aimed at using rugby to make an impact upon communities both socially and economically.[[234]](#cite_note-auto8-234)

Programmes include schools workshops, player visits, discounted rugby kits and rugby camps.[[234]](#cite_note-auto8-234)

### Age Group Rugby and Academy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Age Group Rugby and Academy")]

At Under 16s level, two representative teams are selected (North and South) to compete in the RAG U16 Championship.[[235]](#cite_note-235) At Under 18s level, Cardiff Rugby U18 compete in the RAG U18 league against similar teams from Scarlets, Ospreys, Dragons and RGC. Current Cardiff U18 Head Coach is Chad Mutyambizi.[[236]](#cite_note-236)

High performing players from the U18 squad are offered Cardiff academy contracts. In recent years, the club has begun offering academy contracts to players at a younger age in an effort to retain players targeted by private schools and clubs in England.[[237]](#cite_note-237)

The current Cardiff Academy manager is Gruff Rees who also serves as Director of Rugby of the Cardiff RFC Welsh Premiership team. Past graduates of the Cardiff academy system include [Sam Warburton](/wiki/Sam_Warburton "Sam Warburton"), [Jamie Roberts](/wiki/Jamie_Roberts "Jamie Roberts"), [Tomos Williams](/wiki/Tomos_Williams "Tomos Williams"), [Leigh Halfpenny](/wiki/Leigh_Halfpenny "Leigh Halfpenny"), [Ellis Jenkins](/wiki/Ellis_Jenkins "Ellis Jenkins"), [Josh Navidi](/wiki/Josh_Navidi "Josh Navidi"), and [Rhys Patchell](/wiki/Rhys_Patchell "Rhys Patchell").

## EPCR milestones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: EPCR milestones")]

In 2004 Cardiff Blues received the ERC Elite Award for having played 50 games in the Heineken Cup. This record began in 1995 when Cardiff RFC recorded an away draw at Bordeaux, and continued following the reorganisation of Welsh rugby in 2003.

As of 2022, Cardiff Rugby have played 124 matches in European Competition, making them joint 8th (with [ASM Clermont Auvergne](/wiki/ASM_Clermont_Auvergne "ASM Clermont Auvergne")) on the all-time list.[[251]](#cite_note-Champions_Cup_{{pipe}}_Milestones-258)

[Rhys Williams](/wiki/Rhys_Williams_(rugby_union,_born_1980) "Rhys Williams (rugby union, born 1980)") has made more appearances for Cardiff in the top tier of European Cup Rugby than any other player with 78 appearances. [Martyn Williams](/wiki/Martyn_Williams "Martyn Williams") appeared 85 times in European competition, with 17 of his appearances coming with [Pontypridd RFC](/wiki/Pontypridd_RFC "Pontypridd RFC").[[251]](#cite_note-Champions_Cup_{{pipe}}_Milestones-258)

Rhys Williams is also Cardiff's top try scorer in the competition with 22.[[251]](#cite_note-Champions_Cup_{{pipe}}_Milestones-258)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cardiff_Rugby&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-250)** 11 teams were involved in this season, so one team did not play each week and were awarded 4 points instead. Therefore, each team finished the season with 8 more points than the table would seem to warrant.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-252)** Only 15 rounds were played during the [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Pro14 "2019–20 Pro14") season due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe").[[248]](#cite_note-behind_closed_doors-251)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-254)** Only 16 rounds were played during the [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Pro14 "2020–21 Pro14") season due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe"), with the remaining matches replaced by the [Pro14 Rainbow Cup](/wiki/Pro14_Rainbow_Cup "Pro14 Rainbow Cup") competition.[[249]](#cite_note-253)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-255)** Did not qualify for the 2004–05 Celtic Cup. The tournament was stopped after the 2004–05 season.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-257)** Only 2 rounds of the preliminary stage were played before suspension due to new restrictions surrounding the [COVID-19 pandemic in Europe](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Europe "COVID-19 pandemic in Europe"). A revised format to finish the competition involving the top 8 teams from the preliminary stage and knocked out teams from the [Champions Cup](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "2020–21 European Rugby Champions Cup") was played thereafter.[[250]](#cite_note-256)
